subject: Physics
q: As per the given figure, two plates A and B of thermal conductivity K and 2K are joined together to form a compound plate. The thickness of plates are 4.0 cm and 2.5 cm respectively and the area of cross-section is 120 cm2 for each plate. The equivalent thermal conductivity of the compound plate is 1+5αK, then the value of α will be _____ .
question: As per the given figure, two plates A and B of thermal conductivity K and 2K are joined together to form a compound plate. The thickness of plates are 4.0 cm and 2.5 cm respectively and the area of cross-section is 120 cm2 for each plate. The equivalent thermal conductivity of the compound plate is 1+5αK, then the value of α will be _____ .
solution:
The thermal resistance of rod 1 will be R1=l1KA=4KA and rod 2 will be R2=l22KA=2.52KA.

Since they are connected in series, Req=R1+R2=10.52KA.

If equivalent thermal conductivity is Keq, then Req=l1+l2KeqA=6.5KeqA. Therefore,

10.52KA=6.5KeqA⇒Keq=2621K=1+521K

Hence, α=21.

subject: Physics
q:
A steam engine intakes 50 g of steam at 100°C per minute and cools it down to 20°C. If latent heat of vaporization of steam is 540 cal g-1, then the heat rejected by the steam engine per minute is _____ ×103 cal

(Given : specific heat capacity of water : 1 cal g-1C∘-1)
question:
A steam engine intakes 50 g of steam at 100°C per minute and cools it down to 20°C. If latent heat of vaporization of steam is 540 cal g-1, then the heat rejected by the steam engine per minute is _____ ×103 cal

(Given : specific heat capacity of water : 1 cal g-1C∘-1)
solution:
Heat rejected by the steam engine per minute will be equal to latent heat per minute plus heat rejected by the water to cool down.

∆H∆t=∆m∆tL+∆m∆ts∆T⇒∆H∆t=50×540+50×1×80=31000 cal min-1=31×103 cal min-1

subject: Physics
q: An ice cube of dimensions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in an insulation box of wall thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The box keeping the ice cube at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> of temperature is brought to a room of temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The rate of melting of ice is approximately: (Latent heat of fusion of ice is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and thermal conducting of insulation wall is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
question: An ice cube of dimensions <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi><mo>×</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math> is placed in an insulation box of wall thickness <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cm</mi></math>. The box keeping the ice cube at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> of temperature is brought to a room of temperature <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. The rate of melting of ice is approximately: (Latent heat of fusion of ice is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>3</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>4</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">J</mi><msup><mrow><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></mrow><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math> and thermal conducting of insulation wall is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>05</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">W</mi><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>1</mn></mrow></msup></math>)
solution:
Using the equation of conduction,

dQdt=KAΔTl

Now the total area of the box will be,

A=20.6×0.5+0.5×0.2+0.2×0.6

A=20.3+0.1+0.12

A=20.52=1.04 m2

Therefore,

dQdt=KAΔTl=0.05×1.04×400.01=208 J s-1

This heat will melt the ice. Therefore,

dQdt=dmdtL⇒208=dmdt×3.4×105⇒dmdt=61×10-5 kg s-1

subject: Physics
q: A wooden wheel of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is made of two semicircular parts (see figure); The two parts are held together by a ring made of a metal strip of cross sectional area S and length $L$. $L$ is slightly less than $2 \pi R$. To fit the ring on the wheel, it is heated so that its temperature rises by $\Delta \mathrm{T}$ and it just steps over the wheel. As it cools down to surrounding temperature, it presses the semicircular parts together. If the coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is $\alpha$, and its Youngs' modulus is $Y$, the force that one part of the wheel applies on the other part is :
question: A wooden wheel of radius $\mathrm{R}$ is made of two semicircular parts (see figure); The two parts are held together by a ring made of a metal strip of cross sectional area S and length $L$. $L$ is slightly less than $2 \pi R$. To fit the ring on the wheel, it is heated so that its temperature rises by $\Delta \mathrm{T}$ and it just steps over the wheel. As it cools down to surrounding temperature, it presses the semicircular parts together. If the coefficient of linear expansion of the metal is $\alpha$, and its Youngs' modulus is $Y$, the force that one part of the wheel applies on the other part is :
solution: If temperature increases by $\Delta \mathrm{T}$, Increase in length $\mathrm{L}, \Delta \mathrm{L}=\mathrm{L} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$ $\therefore \quad \frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}=\alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$ Let tension developed in the ring is $\mathrm{T}$. $\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{T}}{\mathrm{S}}=\mathrm{Y} \frac{\Delta \mathrm{L}}{\mathrm{L}}=\mathrm{Y} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$ $\therefore \quad \mathrm{T}=\mathrm{S} \mathrm{Y} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$ From FBD of one part of the wheel, $\mathrm{F}=2 \mathrm{T}$ Where, $F$ is the force that one part of the wheel applies on the other part. $\therefore \mathrm{F}=2 \mathrm{SY} \alpha \Delta \mathrm{T}$

subject: Physics
q:
The heat extracted out of $x$ gram of water initially at $50°C$ to cool it down to $0°C$ is sufficient to evaporate $(1000 - x)$ gram of water also initially at $50°C$. The value of $x$ (closest integer) is _______.
(Take latent heat of water $2256\text{ kJ/kg.K}$, specific heat capacity of water $4200\text{ J/kg.K}$)
question:
The heat extracted out of $x$ gram of water initially at $50°C$ to cool it down to $0°C$ is sufficient to evaporate $(1000 - x)$ gram of water also initially at $50°C$. The value of $x$ (closest integer) is _______.
(Take latent heat of water $2256\text{ kJ/kg.K}$, specific heat capacity of water $4200\text{ J/kg.K}$)
solution:
Heat extracted to cool $x$ gram of water from $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $0^{\circ}\text{C}$ is given by:

$Q_1 = m_1 c \Delta T_1$

$Q_1 = x \times 4.2 \times (50 - 0) = 210x \text{ J}$

Heat required to raise the temperature of $(1000 - x)$ gram of water from $50^{\circ}\text{C}$ to $100^{\circ}\text{C}$ and evaporate it is given by:

$Q_2 = m_2 c \Delta T_2 + m_2 L$

$Q_2 = (1000 - x) \times [4.2 \times (100 - 50) + 2256]$

$Q_2 = (1000 - x) \times (210 + 2256) = 2466(1000 - x) \text{ J}$

Equating the heat extracted and the heat required:

$Q_1 = Q_2$

$210x = 2466(1000 - x)$

$210x = 2466000 - 2466x$

$2676x = 2466000$

$x = \dfrac{2466000}{2676} \approx 921.52$

The closest integer value for $x$ is $922$.

Answer: $922$

subject: Physics
q:
At 40°C, a brass wire of 1 mm radius is hung from the ceiling. A small mass, M is hung from the free end of the wire. When the wire is cooled down from 40°C  to 20°C it regains its original length of 0.2 m. The value of M is close to:
(Coefficient of linear expansion and Young’s modulus of brass are 10-5/°C and 1011 N/m2, respectively; g=10 m s-2 )
question:
At 40°C, a brass wire of 1 mm radius is hung from the ceiling. A small mass, M is hung from the free end of the wire. When the wire is cooled down from 40°C  to 20°C it regains its original length of 0.2 m. The value of M is close to:
(Coefficient of linear expansion and Young’s modulus of brass are 10-5/°C and 1011 N/m2, respectively; g=10 m s-2 )
solution:
Mg=AYlΔl
Δll=αΔT
Mg=AYαΔT
M=AYαΔTg=π×0.0012×1011×10-5×2010=2π
Closest value of M is 9 kg.

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment, a sphere of aluminium of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is heated up to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . Immediately, it is put into water of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cc</mi><mo> </mo></math>at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>27</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> kept in a calorimeter of water equivalent to<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>025</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> . The final temperature of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . The specific heat of the aluminium is(take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Joule</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>calorie</mi></math> )
question: In an experiment, a sphere of aluminium of mass <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> is heated up to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . Immediately, it is put into water of volume <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>150</mn><mo> </mo><mi>cc</mi><mo> </mo></math>at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>27</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> kept in a calorimeter of water equivalent to<math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo> </mo><mn>0</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>025</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> . The final temperature of the system is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msup><mn>40</mn><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> . The specific heat of the aluminium is(take <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>4</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>2</mn><mo> </mo><mi>Joule</mi><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>calorie</mi></math> )
solution:
Let S be the specific heat of aluminium

Q given = Q used

⇒1000×0.2×S×(150−40)=150×1×(40−27)+25×1×(40−27)

⇒200×S×110=150×13+25×13

⇒S=13×175200×110=0.1034 cal g-1 °C

⇒S=103.4 ×4.2 J kg-1 °C=434 J kg-1°C

subject: Physics
q: An external pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is applied on a cube at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> so that it is equally compressed from all sides. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is the bulk modulus of the material of the cube and <math><mi>α</mi></math> is its coefficient of linear expansion. Suppose we want to bring the cube to its original size by heating. The temperature should be raised by:
question: An external pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>P</mi></math> is applied on a cube at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>0</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> so that it is equally compressed from all sides. <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mi>K</mi></math> is the bulk modulus of the material of the cube and <math><mi>α</mi></math> is its coefficient of linear expansion. Suppose we want to bring the cube to its original size by heating. The temperature should be raised by:
solution:
B=-PdVV

dVV=-PB

dV=-PVB

ΔV=VγΔTΔVV=γΔT

ΔVV=3αΔT

K=PΔVV=P3αΔT

ΔT=P3αk

q:
The graph between two temperature scales P and Q is shown in the figure. Between upper fixed point and lower fixed point there are 150 equal divisions of scale P and 100 divisions on scale Q. The relationship for conversion between the two scales is given by :

.
question:
The graph between two temperature scales P and Q is shown in the figure. Between upper fixed point and lower fixed point there are 150 equal divisions of scale P and 100 divisions on scale Q. The relationship for conversion between the two scales is given by :

.
solution:
From the graph

For scale P,
Lower fixed point=180-150=30°P

Upper fixed point=180°P

For scale Q,
Lower fixed point=0°Q

Upper fixed point=100°Q

Thus, the relationship between the two scales P and Q is given by

tP-30180-30=tQ-0100-0

⇒tP-30150=tQ100

subject: Physics
q: A metallic sphere cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If atmospheric temperature around is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, then the sphere's temperature after the nest <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> minutes will be close to:
question: A metallic sphere cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>50</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>300</mn><mo> </mo><mi mathvariant="normal">s</mi></math>. If atmospheric temperature around is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>20</mn><mo> </mo><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>, then the sphere's temperature after the nest <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>5</mn></math> minutes will be close to:
solution:
Using Newton's Law of cooling

50-405 min=K50+402-20     ......(i)

Next 5 min.

40-θ5=K40+θ2-20     .....(ii)

Dividing (ii)/(i)

40-θ10=40+θ-4050+40-40=θ50

40-θ=θ5

200-5θ=θ

∴   θ=2006=33.3 °C

subject: Physics
q: Rods $x$ and $y$ of equal dimensions but of different materials are joined as shown in figure. Temperatures of end points $A$ and $F$ are maintained at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ respectively. Given the thermal conductivity of $\operatorname{rod} x$ is three times of that of $\operatorname{rod} y$, the temperature at junction points $B$ and $E$ are (close to):
question: Rods $x$ and $y$ of equal dimensions but of different materials are joined as shown in figure. Temperatures of end points $A$ and $F$ are maintained at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and $40^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ respectively. Given the thermal conductivity of $\operatorname{rod} x$ is three times of that of $\operatorname{rod} y$, the temperature at junction points $B$ and $E$ are (close to):
solution:
Let $\left[ R = \dfrac{\ell}{3KA} \right]$

$\left[ H = \dfrac{100 - 40}{\dfrac{11R}{2}} \right]$ ...(1)
$H = \dfrac{100 - T_B}{R}$ .....(2)
$H = \dfrac{T_E - 40}{3R}$ .....(3)
using (1) and (2)
$120 = 1100 - 11T_A$
$T_B = 89°C$
using (1) and (3)
$T_E = 73°C$

subject: Physics
q: The temperature of two outer surfaces of a composite slab, consisting of two materials having coefficients of thermal conductivity $K$ and $2 K$ and thickness $x$ and $4 x$, respectively are $T_2$ and $T_1\left(T_2>T_1\right)$. The rate of heat transfer through the slab, in a steady state is $\left(\frac{A\left(T_2-T_1\right) K}{x}\right) f$, with $f$ equal to
question: The temperature of two outer surfaces of a composite slab, consisting of two materials having coefficients of thermal conductivity $K$ and $2 K$ and thickness $x$ and $4 x$, respectively are $T_2$ and $T_1\left(T_2>T_1\right)$. The rate of heat transfer through the slab, in a steady state is $\left(\frac{A\left(T_2-T_1\right) K}{x}\right) f$, with $f$ equal to
solution:
$\Delta \mathrm{q}=\frac{\mathrm{kA}}{\mathrm{x}}\left[\mathrm{T}_2-\frac{2 \mathrm{~T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1}{3}\right]$
$=\frac{\mathrm{kA}}{3 \mathrm{x}}\left[\mathrm{T}_2-\mathrm{T}_1\right]$

question: Two spheres of the same material have radii $1 \mathrm{~m}$ and $4 \mathrm{~m}$ and temperatures $4000 \mathrm{~K}$ and $2000 \mathrm{~K}$ respectively. The ratio of the energy radiated per second by the first sphere to that by the second is

subject: Physics
q: A uniform cylindrical rod of length <math><mi>L</mi></math> and radius r, is made from a material whose Young’s modulus of Elasticity equals <math><mi>Y</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When this rod is heated by temperature T and simultaneously subjected to a net longutudinal compressional force F, its length remains unchanged. The coefficient of volume expansion, of the material of the rod, is (nearly) equal to:
question: A uniform cylindrical rod of length <math><mi>L</mi></math> and radius r, is made from a material whose Young’s modulus of Elasticity equals <math><mi>Y</mi><mo>.</mo></math> When this rod is heated by temperature T and simultaneously subjected to a net longutudinal compressional force F, its length remains unchanged. The coefficient of volume expansion, of the material of the rod, is (nearly) equal to:
solution:
Stress =FA
YαT=FA
α=FAYT
γ=3α=3FAYT
γ=3Fπr2YT

subject: Physics
q: Two conductors have the same resistance at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ but their temperature coefficients of resistance are $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$. The respective temperature coefficients of their series and parallel combinations are nearly
question: Two conductors have the same resistance at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ but their temperature coefficients of resistance are $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$. The respective temperature coefficients of their series and parallel combinations are nearly
solution:
Let $R_0$ be the initial resistance of both conductors $\therefore \quad$ At temperature $\theta$ their resistance will be, $R_1=R_0\left(1+\alpha_1 \theta\right)$ and $R_2=R_0\left(1+\alpha_2 \theta\right)$
for, series combination, $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{s}}=\mathrm{R}_1+\mathrm{R}_2$ $R_{s 0}\left(1+\alpha_s \theta\right)=R_0\left(1+\alpha_1 \theta\right)+R_0\left(1+\alpha_2 \theta\right)$
where $\mathrm{R}_{\mathrm{s} 0}=\mathrm{R}_0+\mathrm{R}_0=2 \mathrm{R}_0$
$\therefore \quad 2 \mathrm{R}_0\left(1+\alpha_s \theta\right)=2 \mathrm{R}_0+\mathrm{R}_0 \theta\left(\alpha_1+\alpha_2\right)$
or $\quad \alpha_s=\frac{\alpha_1+\alpha_2}{2}$
for parallel combination,
$$
R_p=\frac{R_1 R_2}{R_1+R_2}
$$
where, $R_{p 0}=\frac{R_0 R_0}{R_0+R_0}=\frac{R_0}{2}$
$\therefore \quad \frac{\mathrm{R}_0}{2}\left(1+\alpha_{\mathrm{p}} \theta\right)=\frac{\mathrm{R}_0^2\left(1+\alpha_1 \theta+\alpha_2 \theta+\alpha_1 \alpha_2 \theta\right)}{\mathrm{R}_0\left(2+\alpha_1 \theta+\alpha_2 \theta\right)}$
as $\alpha_1$ and $\alpha_2$ are small quantities
$\therefore \quad \alpha_1 \alpha_2$ is negligible
or $\quad \alpha_{\mathrm{p}}=\frac{\alpha_1+\alpha_2}{2+\left(\alpha_1+\alpha_2\right) \theta}=\frac{\alpha_1+\alpha_2}{2}\left[1-\left(\alpha_1+\alpha_2\right) \theta\right]$
as $\left(\alpha_1+\alpha_2\right)^2$ is negligible
$\therefore \quad \alpha_p=\frac{\alpha_1+\alpha_2}{2}$

subject: Physics
q: On a linear temperature scale $\mathrm{Y}$, water freezes at $-160^{\circ} \mathrm{Y}$ and boils at $-50^{\circ} \mathrm{Y}$. On this $\mathrm{Y}$ scale, a temperature of $340 \mathrm{~K}$ would be read as : (water freezes at $273 \mathrm{~K}$ and boils at $373 \mathrm{~K}$ )
question: On a linear temperature scale $\mathrm{Y}$, water freezes at $-160^{\circ} \mathrm{Y}$ and boils at $-50^{\circ} \mathrm{Y}$. On this $\mathrm{Y}$ scale, a temperature of $340 \mathrm{~K}$ would be read as : (water freezes at $273 \mathrm{~K}$ and boils at $373 \mathrm{~K}$ )
solution:
Reading on any scale - LFP
UFP - LFP
$=$ constant for all scales
$\frac{340-273}{373-273}=\frac{{ }^{\circ} \mathrm{y}-(-160)}{-50-(-160)}$
$\Rightarrow \frac{67}{100}=\frac{\mathrm{y}+160}{110}$
$\therefore \quad y=-86.3^{\circ} \mathrm{y}$

subject: Physics
q: The temperature of a metal strip having coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha$ is increased from $T_1$ to $T_2$ resulting in increase of its length by $\Delta L_1$. The temperature is further increased from $T_2$ to $T_3$ such that the increase in its length is $\Delta L_2$. Given $T_3+T_1=2T_2$ and $T_2-T_1=\Delta T$, the value of $\Delta L_2$ is ______.
question: The temperature of a metal strip having coefficient of linear expansion $\alpha$ is increased from $T_1$ to $T_2$ resulting in increase of its length by $\Delta L_1$. The temperature is further increased from $T_2$ to $T_3$ such that the increase in its length is $\Delta L_2$. Given $T_3+T_1=2T_2$ and $T_2-T_1=\Delta T$, the value of $\Delta L_2$ is ______.
solution:
Let the length of the metal strip at temperature $T_1$ be $L_1$.

When the temperature is increased from $T_1$ to $T_2$, the new length $L_2$ is given by:

$L_2 = L_1(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$

where $\Delta T = T_2 - T_1$.

The increase in length is:

$\Delta L_1 = L_2 - L_1 = L_1 \alpha \Delta T$

When the temperature is further increased from $T_2$ to $T_3$, the new length $L_3$ is:

$L_3 = L_2(1 + \alpha \Delta T')$

where $\Delta T' = T_3 - T_2$.

Given $T_3 + T_1 = 2T_2$, we have $T_3 - T_2 = T_2 - T_1 = \Delta T$.

Thus, $\Delta T' = \Delta T$.

The increase in length for this second temperature change is:

$\Delta L_2 = L_3 - L_2 = L_2 \alpha \Delta T$

Substituting $L_2 = L_1(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$ into the equation for $\Delta L_2$:

$\Delta L_2 = L_1(1 + \alpha \Delta T) \alpha \Delta T$

$\Delta L_2 = (L_1 \alpha \Delta T)(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$

Since $\Delta L_1 = L_1 \alpha \Delta T$, substituting this gives:

$\Delta L_2 = \Delta L_1(1 + \alpha \Delta T)$

Answer: $\Delta L_1[1+\alpha\Delta T]$

subject: Physics
q: A large cylindrical rod of length $L$ is made by joining two identical rods of copper and steel of length $\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)$ each. The rods are completely insulated from the surroundings. If the free end of copper rod is maintained at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and that of steel at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ then the temperature of junction is (Thermal conductivity of copper is 9 times that of steel)
question: A large cylindrical rod of length $L$ is made by joining two identical rods of copper and steel of length $\left(\frac{L}{2}\right)$ each. The rods are completely insulated from the surroundings. If the free end of copper rod is maintained at $100^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ and that of steel at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ then the temperature of junction is (Thermal conductivity of copper is 9 times that of steel)
solution: Let conductivity of steel $K_{\text {steel }}=k$ then from question Conductivity of copper $K_{\text {copper }}=9 k$ $$ \begin{aligned} & \theta_{\text {copper }}=100^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\ & \theta_{\text {steel }}=0^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \\ & l_{\text {steel }}=l_{\text {copper }}=\frac{L}{2} \end{aligned} $$ From formula temperature of junction; $$ \begin{aligned} \theta & =\frac{K_{\text {copper }} \theta_{\text {copper }} l_{\text {steel }}+K_{\text {steel }} \theta_{\text {steel }} l_{\text {copper }}}{K_{\text {copper }} l_{\text {steel }}+K_{\text {steel }} l_{\text {copper }}} \\ & =\frac{9 k \times 100 \times \frac{L}{2}+k \times 0 \times \frac{L}{2}}{9 k \times \frac{L}{2}+k \times \frac{L}{2}} \\ & =\frac{\frac{900}{2} k L}{\frac{10 k L}{2}}=90^{\circ} \mathrm{C} \end{aligned} $$

subject: Physics
q: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> grams of steam at <math><msup><mrow><mn>100</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is mixed with <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of ice at its melting point in a thermally insulated container. If it produces liquid water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>40</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> [heat of vaporization of water is <math><mn>540</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> and heat of fusion of ice is <math><mn>80</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> ], the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is ________
question: <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> grams of steam at <math><msup><mrow><mn>100</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is mixed with <math><mn>200</mn><mi mathvariant="normal"></mi><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></math> of ice at its melting point in a thermally insulated container. If it produces liquid water at <math><msup><mrow><mn>40</mn></mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">o</mi></mrow></msup><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> [heat of vaporization of water is <math><mn>540</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> and heat of fusion of ice is <math><mn>80</mn><mrow><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">c</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">a</mi><mi mathvariant="normal">l</mi></mrow><mo>/</mo><mrow><mi mathvariant="normal">g</mi></mrow></mrow></math> ], the value of <math><mi mathvariant="normal">M</mi></math> is ________
solution:
MiceLf+Mice40-0Cw=MsteamLv+Msteam100-40Cw
⇒20080+401=M540+601
⇒200120=M600
M=40 gm

subject: Physics
q: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of water at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is converted into steam at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> by boiling at atmospheric pressure. The volume of water changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> as a liquid to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>671</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> as steam. The change in internal energy of the system during the process will be (Given latent heat of vaporisation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>2257</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, Atmospheric pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open=""><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
question: <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kg</mi></math> of water at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> is converted into steam at <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>100</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> by boiling at atmospheric pressure. The volume of water changes from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>00</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mrow><mo>-</mo><mn>3</mn></mrow></msup><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> as a liquid to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>1</mn><mo>.</mo><mn>671</mn><mo> </mo><msup><mi mathvariant="normal">m</mi><mn>3</mn></msup></math> as steam. The change in internal energy of the system during the process will be (Given latent heat of vaporisation <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>=</mo><mn>2257</mn><mo> </mo><mi>kJ</mi><mo>/</mo><mi>kg</mi></math>, Atmospheric pressure <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mfenced open=""><mrow><mo>=</mo><mn>1</mn><mo>×</mo><msup><mn>10</mn><mn>5</mn></msup><mo> </mo><mi>Pa</mi></mrow></mfenced></math>
solution:
The work to be done in the process is given by

dW=PdV=1×105 Pa×(1.671-0.001) m3=1.670×105 J

The change in heat energy during the vaporisation process can be calculated as follows-

ΔQsupplied =2257×1×103 J=22.57×105 J

Hence, the change in internal energy in the process is given by

ΔU=ΔQsupplied-ΔW=(22.57-1.67)×105 J=20.9×105 J=2090 kJ

subject: Physics
q: Temperature difference of 120oC is maintained between two ends of a uniform rod AB of length 2L. Another bent rod PQ, of same cross-section as AB and length 3L2, is connected across AB (See figure). In steady state, temperature difference between P and Q will be close to:
question: Temperature difference of 120oC is maintained between two ends of a uniform rod AB of length 2L. Another bent rod PQ, of same cross-section as AB and length 3L2, is connected across AB (See figure). In steady state, temperature difference between P and Q will be close to:
solution:
T1-T2=120oC

We define thermal resistance

R=LKA

Given circuit can be reduced to

Equivalent resistance Req=8R5

Thermal current i=T1-T28R5

TP-TQ=T1-T28R5 3R5

=38×120=45

subject: Physics
q: When the temperature of a metal wire is increased from 0ºC to 10ºC, its length increases by 0.02%.The percentage change in its mass density will be closed to:
question: When the temperature of a metal wire is increased from 0ºC to 10ºC, its length increases by 0.02%.The percentage change in its mass density will be closed to:
solution:
Δℓ=ℓαΔt
α=ΔℓℓΔT=0.02100×10;  α=2×10-5
γ=3α=6×10-5
ΔVV=γ×ΔT;  ΔVV×100=6×10-5×10×100=6×10-2
Volume increase by 0.06% therefore density decrease by 0.06%

subject: Physics
q: In an experiment to verify Newton's law of cooling. a graph is plotted between. the temperature difference ΔT of the water and surroundings and time as shown in figure. The initial temperature of water is taken as 80°C. The value of t2 as mentioned in the graph will be
question: In an experiment to verify Newton's law of cooling. a graph is plotted between. the temperature difference ΔT of the water and surroundings and time as shown in figure. The initial temperature of water is taken as 80°C. The value of t2 as mentioned in the graph will be
solution:
According to the Newton's law of cooling, -dTdt=KT-T0.

Applying approximation, -∆T∆t=KT1+T22-T0 

For first 6 min, T1=80°C, T2=40+20=60°C, T0=20°C

⇒-60-806=K70-20⇒K=-60-806×70-20

⇒K=206×50=115

For 6 to t2, T1=60°C, T2=20+20=40°C, T0=20°C

-60-80t2-6=K50-20⇒t2-6=15×2030⇒t2=16 min

subject: Physics
q: The thermo emf of a thermocouple varies with the temperature $\theta$ of the hot junction as $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{a} \theta+\mathrm{b} \theta^2$ in volts where the ratio $\mathrm{a} / \mathrm{b}$ is $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the cold junction is kept at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then the neutral temperature is
question: The thermo emf of a thermocouple varies with the temperature $\theta$ of the hot junction as $\mathrm{E}=\mathrm{a} \theta+\mathrm{b} \theta^2$ in volts where the ratio $\mathrm{a} / \mathrm{b}$ is $700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. If the cold junction is kept at $0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$, then the neutral temperature is
options: no neutral temperature is possible for this thermocouple.
solution:
$E=a \theta+b \theta^2$
At neutral temperature $\mathrm{dE} / \mathrm{d} \theta=0$
$\therefore \frac{\mathrm{dE}}{\mathrm{d} \theta}=\mathrm{a}+2 \mathrm{~b} \theta_{\mathrm{n}}=0 ; \theta_{\mathrm{n}}=-\frac{\mathrm{a}}{2 \mathrm{~b}}$
Now $\frac{a}{b}=700^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ (given)
$\theta_n=-700 / 2=-350^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
Now $\theta_c=0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.
So, $\theta_n>0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$
But mathematically $\theta_{\mathrm{n}} < 0^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$.

subject: Physics
q: The ratio of the coefficient of volume expansion of a glass container to that of a viscous liquid kept inside the container is $1: 4$. What fraction of the inner volume of the container should the liquid occupy so that the volume of the remaining vacant space will be same at all temperatures ?
question: The ratio of the coefficient of volume expansion of a glass container to that of a viscous liquid kept inside the container is $1: 4$. What fraction of the inner volume of the container should the liquid occupy so that the volume of the remaining vacant space will be same at all temperatures ?
solution:
When there is no change in liquid level in vessel then $\gamma_{\text {real }}^{\prime}=\gamma^{\prime}$ vessel Change in volume in liquid relative to vessel
$$
\Delta \mathrm{V}_{\mathrm{app}}=\mathrm{V} \gamma_{\text {app }}^{\prime} \Delta \theta=\mathrm{V}\left(\gamma_{\text {real }}^{\prime}-\gamma_{\text {vessel }}^{\prime}\right)
$$

subject: Physics
q: A bowl filled with very hot soup cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>98</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to $86^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 2 minutes when the room temperature is $22^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How long it will take to cool from $75^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $69^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
question: A bowl filled with very hot soup cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>98</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to $86^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ in 2 minutes when the room temperature is $22^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$. How long it will take to cool from $75^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ to $69^{\circ} \mathrm{C}$ ?
solution:
From Newton's law of cooling, we have

ΔQΔt=-KTavg-T0

Where, Q=ms∆T, m is the mass, s is the specific heat and ∆T is the temperature change.

Case 1:

∆t=2 minutes, T0=22°C

ms×122=-K98+862-22

6=-Kms70    ...1

Case 2:

ms×6Δt=-K75+692-22

6Δt=-Kms(50)   ...2

Dividing (2) by (1), we get

⇒6Δt(6)=5070

⇒Δt=75=1.4 min

subject: Physics
q: A cylinder of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> is surrounded by a cylindrical shell of inner radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> and outer radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. The thermal conductivity of the material of the inner cylinder is <math><msub><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and that of the outer cylinder is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. Assuming no loss of heat, the effective thermal conductivity of the system for heat flowing along the length of the cylinder is:
question: A cylinder of radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> is surrounded by a cylindrical shell of inner radius <math><mi>R</mi></math> and outer radius <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>2</mn><mi>R</mi></math>. The thermal conductivity of the material of the inner cylinder is <math><msub><mrow><mi>K</mi></mrow><mrow><mn>1</mn></mrow></msub></math> and that of the outer cylinder is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><msub><mi>K</mi><mn>2</mn></msub></math>. Assuming no loss of heat, the effective thermal conductivity of the system for heat flowing along the length of the cylinder is:
solution:
Let q1 and q2 be the rate of flow of heat through inner part from outer part respecting net flow of heat


q=q1+q2

=K1πR2l.ΔT+K2.π2R2-R2l.ΔT

=πR2lΔTK1+3K2

If the cylinder is replaced by a single material of thermal conductivity K then

q=K.2R2l.ΔT

On comparison,

K=K1+3K24.

subject: Physics
q:
Due to cold weather, a 1 m water pipe of cross-sectional area 1 cm2 is filled with ice at -10 °C. Resistive heating is used to melt the ice. Current of 0.5 A is passed through 4 kΩ resistance. Assuming that all the heat produced is used for melting, what is the minimum time required?

(Given latent heat of fusion for water/ice=3.33×105 J kg-1, specific heat of ice =2×103 J kg-1 °C-1 and density of ice =103 kg m-3 )
question:
Due to cold weather, a 1 m water pipe of cross-sectional area 1 cm2 is filled with ice at -10 °C. Resistive heating is used to melt the ice. Current of 0.5 A is passed through 4 kΩ resistance. Assuming that all the heat produced is used for melting, what is the minimum time required?

(Given latent heat of fusion for water/ice=3.33×105 J kg-1, specific heat of ice =2×103 J kg-1 °C-1 and density of ice =103 kg m-3 )
solution:
The mass of ice, m=ρAL=103×10-4×1=10-1 kg, 

Energy required to melt the ice, =mSΔT+mL, 

⇒i2Rt=mSΔT+mL
⇒(1)2×103×t=103×1×10-4×2000×10+103×1×10-4×330×103⇒(1)2×103×t=3.53×104⇒t=35.3 s

subject: Physics
q: A body cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> minutes. If, temperature of surroundings is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Then, after the next <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> minutes, its temperature will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.
question: A body cools from <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>60</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> to <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>40</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math> in <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> minutes. If, temperature of surroundings is <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>10</mn><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>. Then, after the next <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mn>6</mn></math> minutes, its temperature will be ______ <math xmlns="http://www.w3.org/1998/Math/MathML"><mo>°</mo><mi mathvariant="normal">C</mi></math>.
solution:
From Newton’s law of cooling

dTdt=kTavg-T0, here, T0 is surrounding temperature.

For case 1:

60-406=k60+402-10

206=k50-10    ...1 

For case 2:

40-T6=k40+T2-10    ...2 

From equation 1, we have k=112

Putting in equation 2

40-T6=11240+T2-10

⇒10+T2=80-2T⇒2T+T2=70

⇒T=1405=28°C

subject: Physics
q: Assuming the sun to be a spherical body of radius $\mathrm{R}$ at a temperature of $\mathrm{T} \mathrm{K}$, evaluate the total radiant power, incident on Earth, at a distance $r$ from the Sun.
question: Assuming the sun to be a spherical body of radius $\mathrm{R}$ at a temperature of $\mathrm{T} \mathrm{K}$, evaluate the total radiant power, incident on Earth, at a distance $r$ from the Sun.
solution: $\frac{\pi r_0^2}{4 \pi r^2}\left(\sigma \mathrm{T}^4 \cdot 4 \pi \mathrm{R}^2\right)=\frac{\sigma \pi \mathrm{T}^4 \mathrm{R}^2 \mathrm{r}_0^2}{\mathrm{r}^2}$
